claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the **child's age**. Could you tell me:

**How old is the child you're claiming for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

This helps determine which eligibility path applies to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my daughter Alex - she's 14 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Since Alex is 14 years old, she falls into the "under 16" category, which is great news as children under 16 automatically qualify on the age requirement.

Let me navigate to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about Alex's work status:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

At 14 years old, most children aren't in regular paid employment, but I need to confirm this. Is Alex:
- Not working, or
- Working fewer than 24 hours per week in a paid job?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't work at all. She's just in school like any normal 14-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes sense! Since Alex isn't working at all, she meets this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in her own name?**

Specifically, this refers to benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

At 14 and in school, it's very unlikely she'd be receiving these, but I just need to confirm - is Alex free from receiving any of these benefits in her own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she doesn't get any benefits in her own name. She's just a regular kid - I don't think 14-year-olds usually get those kinds of benefits anyway!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: You're absolutely right! It's quite rare for a 14-year-old to receive those benefits - just need to check all the boxes. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex currently on an apprenticeship programme?**

Since you mentioned she's just in school, I'm guessing she's not, but I need to confirm. Is Alex:
- **Not in any apprenticeship** (just in regular school), or
- In some form of apprenticeship programme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, she's not doing an apprenticeship - she's just in regular school. She's only 14, so that's a bit young for apprenticeships anyway!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! At 14, she's just focused on her regular schooling. Let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Which of these applies to you? For most people, it's simply that they **normally live in the UK**.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment, and I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad and don't fall under the Crown servant category, I need to check if any of the other residency paths might apply to you.

Just to be thorough before I make a determination:

**Do you have any of the following?**

- **Settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme (even while living abroad)?
- **Pre-settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme with any connection to the UK (working, studying, or as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national)?

If none of these apply to you, I'm afraid you wouldn't meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't have any of those statuses. I just moved abroad - nothing to do with the EU Settlement Scheme or any special arrangements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, I need to deliver some disappointing news.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on our assessment, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** at this time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary: Not Eligible for Child Benefit

**Child:** Alex (age 14)  
**Result:** ❌ **Not Eligible**

### Reason:
You do not meet the **UK residency requirements** for Child Benefit.

### What this means:
Although Alex herself meets all the criteria for a qualifying child (she's under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship), **you as the claimant** must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances to claim Child Benefit.

### To be eligible, you would need to:
- Normally live in the UK, **or**
- Have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, **or**
- Have pre-settled status with qualifying criteria (working, studying, or sufficient resources), **or**
- Be a Crown servant living abroad, **or**
- Be living in certain qualifying countries with special arrangements

### What you can do:
- If your circumstances change and you **return to live in the UK**, you could then apply for Child Benefit
- If you believe you may qualify under a special arrangement not discussed, you can contact HMRC directly for advice

I'm sorry I couldn't bring better news. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in her own right, and not in an apprenticeship - all of which meet the child-related eligibility criteria. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. They do not normally live in the UK, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not a Crown servant living abroad. Since the claimant fails the residency requirement, they cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: While Alex meets all the requirements for being a qualifying child (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad without any qualifying status such as settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme or Crown servant status.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. They do not normally live in the UK, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not a Crown servant living abroad. Since the claimant fails the residency requirement, they cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: While Alex meets all the requirements for being a qualifying child (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements for Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad without any qualifying status such as settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme or Crown servant status.
